findings.message: GDPR violation: the caller's email address (personal data / PII) is written to application logs at INFO level. Logs typically have a far longer retention than the operational need for this signal, are replicated to centralised log stores, and are not subject to a documented retention or right-to-erasure workflow. Logging raw email also means there is no audit trail of *who* invoked the search and no pseudonymisation — the email is the identifier in the log line itself, conflating operational logs with an audit record.
findings.suggestion: Do not log the raw email. Either drop the identifier entirely, or replace it with a stable pseudonymous id (e.g. a salted HMAC of the email, or a server-side user id) so the value cannot be reversed and is covered by your existing pseudonymous-id retention/erasure policy. If an audit trail of who searched is genuinely required, write it to a dedicated audit sink with a documented retention window and an erasure hook, not to the general application log.

findings.message: Personal data (email) is accepted as a query-string parameter on a POST endpoint. Query strings are routinely captured by access logs, proxies, CDNs and browser history, which puts the email into surfaces that are out of scope of any application-level retention/erasure policy — a GDPR data-minimisation and storage-limitation problem independent of the explicit logger call below.
findings.suggestion: Move the email out of the query string into the JSON request body (e.g. a Pydantic model), or — better — derive the caller identity from the authenticated session/token rather than accepting it from the client at all; if personalisation truly needs an email, hash it client-side or accept an opaque user id.

findings.message: The endpoint performs a personalised, identity-bearing search but has no audit-log entry (the only record is a plain INFO log line, which is not an audit trail: no actor/action/resource/outcome schema, no tamper-evident sink, no documented retention). For an operation keyed on a user identifier this is a GDPR Art. 30 / accountability gap.
findings.suggestion: Emit a structured audit event (actor=pseudonymised user id, action="items.search", resource, outcome, correlation_id) to the dedicated audit channel with a defined retention and an erasure hook, rather than relying on the general application logger.
